step2_rag_chunks: If someone offers you a cigarette, practice politely saying no and reminding yourself why you decided to quit.

step4_session_phase.phase_instruction: You are in the EXPLORATION phase. You know the basics. Now gently explore what situations or emotions make them smoke more — triggers like stress, meals, social settings, boredom. Do NOT suggest any strategies yet. Just understand their situation.

step1_extraction.smoking_status: cigarettes after a long shift or during a break at work

step2_rag_chunks: Smoking is often tied to a habit loop â€” a cue triggers the urge, smoking is the routine, and relief is the reward. Understanding what your specific cues are is the first step. It could be a time of day, a place, an emotion, or even a person. Once we identify the cue, we can work on changing just the routine while keeping the relief.
Many people smoke out of habit during specific routines such as after meals, while drinking tea or coffee, or when talking with friends.
Avoid situations where others are smoking during the early stages of quitting. Being around smokers can increase the urge to smoke.

step3_kg_subgraph.smoking_status: cigarettes after a long shift or during a break at work

turns.user_message: I mean, I know it's not great for me, but I'm not really worried about it. I'm healthy enough, and I figure I'll just deal with any problems when they come up. Besides, I've tried quitting before, and it didn't work out. I don't see why it would be different this time.
step2_rag_chunks: It is completely normal to want to quit and also feel unsure about it at the same time. Most people feel this way â€” one part of them wants to stop, another part is not ready. That conflict is not weakness, it is just how change works. It helps to talk about both sides openly rather than pushing the doubts aside.
Quitting smoking reduces the risk of lung disease, heart disease, and many types of cancer.

If you have tried to quit before, that experience is actually useful information â€” not a failure. What worked even a little? What made it harder? Most people who successfully quit have tried several times before it sticks. Each attempt teaches you something about your own pattern.

Patient: Well, it's just...I love the taste, the sensation of the smoke in my lungs. And it's just something I do after a long shift or during a break at work. It's like a little reward to myself. Plus, all my friends smoke, so it's just part of the social scene, you know?
